pattern_recognition: Linked List Manipulation; this problem involves understanding the structure of linked lists and how to traverse them effectively. Signals include needing to count nodes and perform operations based on their positions.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Traverse the entire list to count the total number of nodes, then traverse again to find the (length - n)th node and remove it.
approaches.algorithm: 1. Count the total number of nodes in the list. 2. Calculate the position of the node to remove. 3. Traverse to that position and remove the node.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use two pointers to find the nth node from the end in a single pass. One pointer advances n nodes ahead, then both pointers move together until the first pointer reaches the end.
approaches.algorithm: 1. Initialize two pointers, both at the head. 2. Move the first pointer n steps ahead. 3. Move both pointers until the first pointer reaches the end. 4. Remove the node pointed to by the second pointer.

dry_run: Example input: 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5, n = 2. In the optimal approach, first pointer moves to 2 (1 -> 2). Then both pointers move together until the first reaches the end (1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5). The second pointer is now at 3, so we remove it, resulting in 1 -> 2 -> 4 -> 5.
edge_cases: Empty list: return an empty list. Single element: removing the only node results in an empty list. Duplicates: ensure the correct node is removed based on position. Large input: performance should be efficient. Overflow: handle cases where n is larger than the list length.
common_pitfalls: 1. Forgetting to handle the case where n equals the length of the list, which requires special handling for the head. 2. Not considering the case of an empty list, which should return an empty list. 3. Incorrectly calculating the position of the node to remove, especially when n is greater than the length of the list.
